exercises.title: Which collection for each problem?
exercises.instructions:
Match each problem to the right collection type. For each, write the correct Java declaration. Four separate questions — just write the correct type (List, Set, or Map) and a one-line reason.

1. Track which userIds have visited a page today. Fast membership check.
2. Store a playlist of songs a user added in order (duplicates allowed).
3. Look up a product's price by its SKU code.
4. Count how many orders each customer has placed.
exercises.expected_solution:
1. Set<String> visitedUsers = new HashSet<>();  // membership, O(1)
2. List<String> playlist = new ArrayList<>();    // ordered, duplicates OK
3. Map<String, Double> priceBysku = new HashMap<>();  // keyed lookup
4. Map<String, Integer> ordersByCustomer = new HashMap<>();  // frequency count
hints: Membership/uniqueness → Set
Ordered sequence → List
Key→value lookup → Map
Frequency counting → Map<K, Integer>

exercises.title: Performance comparison trace
exercises.scenario: Understand O(1) vs O(n) in practice.
hints: Both give the same results — the difference is SPEED (O(n) vs O(1))
3 is in both → true; 9 is in neither → false

exercises.title: Fix the antipattern: List for membership
exercises.scenario: Replace O(n) membership check with O(1) Set.
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;

// Refactor: replace List with Set for O(1) membership check
hints: Membership check → Set<String>
contains() is O(1) on HashSet vs O(n) on ArrayList
Same result, dramatically better performance at scale

exercises.title: Fix the antipattern: List for lookup
exercises.scenario: Replace linear scan with HashMap O(1) lookup.
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;

// Refactor: use HashMap<String, String> for O(1) lookup by ID
hints: Key lookup → Map<String, String>
get('E002') is O(1) vs scanning all employees
Much simpler code too

exercises.title: LinkedHashMap for insertion-order config
exercises.scenario: Preserve insertion order in a map.
exercises.instructions:
Build a config map that preserves insertion order. Show that iterating a LinkedHashMap gives keys in insertion order (HashMap would not).

Insert: timeout=30, retries=3, debug=true

Expected output (in insertion order):
timeout=30
retries=3
debug=true
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;

// Use LinkedHashMap to preserve insertion order
hints: LinkedHashMap preserves insertion order
HashMap would give unpredictable order
entrySet() iterates in insertion order

exercises.title: TreeMap for sorted dictionary
exercises.scenario: When sorted keys matter more than O(1) speed.
exercises.instructions:
Build a small dictionary (word → definition). Print all words alphabetically using TreeMap's natural sorted key order.

Expected output:
apple: a red fruit
banana: a yellow fruit
cherry: a small red fruit
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;

// TreeMap gives sorted keys automatically

test_cases.expected_stdout:
apple: a red fruit
banana: a yellow fruit
cherry: a small red fruit

hints: TreeMap keeps keys in sorted (alphabetical) order automatically
No need to sort after putting entries
Iteration over entrySet() gives alphabetical order

exercises.title: Map of Lists — grouping pattern
exercises.instructions:
Group employees by department using `Map<String, List<String>>`.

Employees: Aisha(Engineering), Raj(Sales), Priya(Engineering), Vikram(HR), Anita(Sales)

Expected output (sorted departments, sorted names within):
Engineering: [Aisha, Priya]
HR: [Vikram]
Sales: [Anita, Raj]
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;

// Map<String, List<String>> — group by department
hints: Map<String, List<String>> — department → list of names
computeIfAbsent creates the list on first use
Sort both the dept keys and each name list for output

exercises.title: Map of TreeSets — sorted unique grouping
exercises.scenario: Nested collections for sorted unique groups.
exercises.instructions:
Build a tag index: Map<String, TreeSet<String>> — each tag maps to a sorted set of article titles that have that tag.

Articles:
- 'Java Guide' with tags: java, programming
- 'Python Intro' with tags: python, programming  
- 'OOP Concepts' with tags: java, oop, programming

Expected output (sorted tags, sorted articles within):
java: [Java Guide, OOP Concepts]
oop: [OOP Concepts]
programming: [Java Guide, OOP Concepts, Python Intro]
python: [Python Intro]
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;

// Map<String, TreeSet<String>> — tag index with sorted unique article sets

test_cases.expected_stdout:
java: [Java Guide, OOP Concepts]
oop: [OOP Concepts]
programming: [Java Guide, OOP Concepts, Python Intro]
python: [Python Intro]

hints: TreeMap<String, TreeSet<String>> — sorted tags, sorted articles per tag
TreeMap keeps tag keys alphabetical
TreeSet keeps article names sorted and unique
No extra sorting needed — the collections handle it

exercises.title: LinkedHashSet for ordered dedup
exercises.instructions:
A user visits pages in this order (with some revisits). Deduplicate while preserving the FIRST-VISIT order.

Visits: /home /about /home /products /about /contact /products /home

Expected output:
[/home, /about, /products, /contact]
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;

// LinkedHashSet preserves insertion order while deduplicating
hints: LinkedHashSet: dedup + insertion order
HashSet would work but order undefined
Convert to ArrayList just for the nice [] print format

exercises.title: Right collection for a library catalog
exercises.scenario: Design multiple data structures for the same domain.
exercises.instructions:
Design structures for a library system. For EACH requirement, use the most appropriate collection:
1. All ISBN codes in the library (unique, fast lookup)
2. Books a reader has borrowed, in order borrowed
3. ISBN → Book object lookup
4. All books per genre, sorted by title within genre

Create all four, add sample data, demonstrate each works.

Expected output:
Has ISBN-001: true
Borrowed in order: [Dune, Foundation]
Found: Clean Code
SciFi titles: [Dune, Foundation]
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;

// Four data structures for four library requirements

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Has ISBN-001: true
Borrowed in order: [Dune, Foundation]
Found: Clean Code
SciFi titles: [Dune, Foundation]

hints: 1 = HashSet (membership), 2 = ArrayList (ordered), 3 = HashMap (lookup), 4 = Map<String, TreeSet<String>>
TreeSet sorts titles automatically within each genre
Each choice justified by the operation it needs to support

exercises.title: Trace TreeMap sorted output
exercises.scenario: TreeMap iteration is always sorted by key.
hints: TreeMap always iterates in sorted key order
Integer keys → numerically ascending: 1, 2, 3, 4
Insertion order (3, 1, 4, 2) doesn't matter

exercises.title: Product inventory — three structures together
exercises.scenario: One domain, three different collection needs.
exercises.instructions:
Product inventory with three requirements:
1. `Map<String, Double>` — price by SKU for O(1) lookup
2. `Map<String, Set<String>>` — which SKUs belong to each category (unique SKUs per category)
3. `TreeMap<Double, String>` — SKU sorted by price (ascending)

Add 4 products and demonstrate all three.

Expected output:
Price of SKU-003: 18999.0
Electronics SKUs: [SKU-001, SKU-002, SKU-003]
Cheapest first: SKU-004(499.0) SKU-001(799.0) SKU-002(1999.0) SKU-003(18999.0)
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;

// Three structures for three needs on the same product data

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Price of SKU-003: 18999.0
Electronics SKUs: [SKU-001, SKU-002, SKU-003]
Cheapest first: SKU-004(499.0) SKU-001(799.0) SKU-002(1999.0) SKU-003(18999.0)

hints: HashMap for price lookup — O(1) by SKU
Map<String, TreeSet> for category — sorted unique SKUs
TreeMap<Double, String> keeps entries sorted by price key
Iterate TreeMap for auto-sorted price output

exercises.title: Refactor antipattern — full rewrite
exercises.scenario: Identify and fix three collection antipatterns in one class.
hints: Antipattern 1: List<String[]> → proper Employee class
Antipattern 2: linear scan for find() → HashMap<String, Employee>
Antipattern 3: List<String> with contains for processedIds → HashSet<String>
The fixed version is simpler AND faster

exercises.title: Hospital patient management — design from requirements
exercises.scenario: Design data structures from scratch for a real domain.
exercises.instructions:
Design data structures for a hospital system:
1. Look up patient by ID (instant)
2. All patients admitted today, in admission order
3. Patients grouped by ward (surgical, medical, ICU) — each ward's list alphabetical by name
4. Discharged patient IDs (for quick 'is this patient discharged?' check)
5. Active ward names (unique)

Create all five, add sample data, demonstrate each.

Expected output:
Patient P001: Aisha (Surgical)
Today's admissions: [P001, P002, P003]
Surgical ward: [Aisha, Priya]
P002 discharged: true
Active wards: 3
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;

// Design all 5 data structures for the hospital system

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Patient P001: Aisha (Surgical)
Today's admissions: [P001, P002, P003, P004]
Surgical ward: [Aisha, Priya]
P002 discharged: true
Active wards: 3

hints: 1=HashMap, 2=ArrayList, 3=Map<String,TreeSet<String>>, 4=HashSet, 5=HashSet
TreeSet keeps ward patient names sorted
Surgical has Aisha and Priya — TreeSet sorts them alphabetically

exercises.title: E-commerce engine — Module 6 full synthesis
exercises.scenario: All collection types in one system, designed correctly.
exercises.instructions:
Build a mini e-commerce search engine using every collection type covered in Module 6:

- `List<Product>` as the master catalog (ordered, browseable)
- `Map<String, Product>` for SKU lookup (O(1))
- `Map<String, List<Product>>` for category grouping
- `Set<String>` for out-of-stock tracking
- `TreeMap<Double, List<Product>>` for price-range browsing (sorted)

Implement: `search(query)` finds products by name (case-insensitive contains). `inPriceRange(min, max)` uses TreeMap subMap.

Expected output:
Total products: 4
Search 'key': [Keyboard]
In range ₹500-₹2000: [Mouse, Keyboard]
Electronics count: 3
Out of stock: 1
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;

// Full Module 6 synthesis — all collection types

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Total products: 4
Search 'key': [Keyboard]
In range ₹500-₹2000: [Java Book, Mouse, Keyboard]
Electronics count: 3
Out of stock: 1

hints: Each collection serves a different query type
TreeMap.subMap(min, true, max, true) gives the price-range slice
subMap returns a view of entries between min and max inclusive
outOfStock is a Set<String> of SKUs — O(1) check
